card.intuition: Designing APIs that are intuitive, scalable, and follow standard web conventions for resources and endpoints.
card.analogy: A clear, well-labeled restaurant menu: you know exactly where the appetizers are, where the main courses are, and how to order more of something vs. sending something back.
when_to_use: Creating the primary interface for your backend services
When you need a standard, predictable way for multiple team's frontends to consume your data
gotchas: Don't use verbs in URLs (e.g., /getUser/123) — use nouns and HTTP verbs instead (GET /users/123)
Always return the correct HTTP status code; don't return '200 OK' with an error message in the JSON body

steps.label: HTTP verbs
steps.annotation: HTTP methods express intent: GET=read, POST=create, PUT=replace, PATCH=update, DELETE=remove.

steps.label: Status codes
steps.annotation: 200 OK, 201 Created, 400 Bad Request, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict.

steps.label: HATEOAS
steps.annotation: Response includes links to related actions — client discovers API dynamically.

steps.label: Versioning
steps.annotation: Version in URL or header — never break existing clients.

exercise.prompt: Correct the non-RESTful URL and choose the right HTTP verb to update just the email of user 42.
hints: The URL should be just the resource: /users/42
A partial update uses the PATCH verb
exercise.test_code:
assert "/users/42" in operation.values()
assert "PATCH" in operation.values()
print("PASS")
